This approach teaches you to look for forcing moves that neutralize threats rather than just racing material. Takeaway: When facing a threat, look for a check that buys you time to improve your position.

I'd play Qd8.

You saw a chance to attack the f3 pawn with your bishop, but that target is small compared to what's available on the queenside. By moving your queen to d8, you create a double attack against White's rook on c7 and pawn on d5 — this forces White to respond to genuine threats rather than chase weak pawns. Your pieces are already well-positioned; this move ties their army down and keeps you in control of the position.

Takeaway: When you can attack two pieces or important targets at once, that double pressure usually outweighs sniping at a single pawn.

I'd play Qb4. You played Bc5, which develops a piece but doesn't address the real features of the position—notice that White's pawn on b3 is undefended, and Qb4 immediately attacks it while also protecting your own knight on d4. At the intermediate level, you want to ask yourself what loose pawns or pieces your opponent has left behind before making routine developing moves. This queen move is active, practical, and forces White to respond to your threat. Takeaway: Before developing, scan for undefended enemy pawns and pieces you can pressure.

I'd play Nc3. Your move Ng5 jumps across the board for a tactical gesture that doesn't quite work, but Nc3 takes the simpler, stronger approach: it develops your queenside knight to a natural outpost, connects your rooks, and prepares to castle. After Nc3, you can follow up with Ne1 to reinforce against Black's knight on h5, steadily improving your position rather than chasing phantoms. This is the kind of calm, completing-development move that advanced players trust.

Takeaway: When the tactics don't quite add up, fall back on sound development — it's almost always the right answer.

I'd play Re2+. You found a good move by pushing your passed pawn with d3, but first let's give check and force the white king toward the corner — this captures a tempo to advance your pawn more safely. After the king moves, you'll still be able to push your passed pawn, but now the white king is in a worse spot. Takeaway: When you have a passed pawn, giving a check first can win time before you commit to pushing it.

I'd play b1=B. You're absolutely right to promote your pawn — getting a new piece is usually a great achievement! But here's a fun twist: when you promote to a queen, White's knight can immediately capture it, and then your king recaptures on b1. If you instead promote to a bishop, White's knight still captures it, but you've saved yourself from accidentally giving White an extra tempo to reposition. After the captures settle, your king can head toward the center and your other pawns can advance. Promoting to a bishop is called "underpromotion," and in this position it's just as effective but slightly cleaner. Takeaway: Always check whether your newly promoted piece can be captured immediately — sometimes a subtler promotion is just as good.

I'd play Qd8. You saw an opportunity to attack with your bishop, but the pawn on f3 isn't the right target — White can simply ignore it and your bishop becomes passive. By moving your queen to d8, you create real pressure with a coordinated attack on both the rook on c7 and the pawn on d5, forcing White to respond to genuine threats. Double attacks like this are far more powerful than sniping at a single pawn. Takeaway: When you can attack two pieces or pawns at once, you're usually on the right track.

I'd play Re2+. Giving check is often useful, and here it serves a clear purpose: after the king moves, your rook is well-positioned to swing behind White's pawn and target it from g2. This is a practical, forcing approach that keeps your opponent responding to you, securing an even endgame position. The student's d3 is fine, but Re2+ shows how to use active piece play in this endgame.

Takeaway: In rook endings, checking to reposition your pieces behind enemy pawns is a reliable plan.

I'd play Nc3. You've moved the same knight three times already this game, which has cost you valuable time—your student's move continues this pattern, bouncing the knight to g5 where it can be chased away. Instead, develop your b1 knight to c3, bringing a new piece into the game and completing your development. From there, you can castle and stop shuffling pieces, letting your active army speak for itself. Takeaway: When you've already moved a piece twice, look for chances to develop your other pieces before moving it again.

I'd play Nc3.

You played Ng5, which sends your knight on an adventure before finishing your development—that knight can be chased away later, but right now your priority should be getting your pieces off the back rank and into the game. Nc3 develops this knight to a good square, connects your rooks, and gets you one step closer to castling to safety. After your pieces are developed and your king is safe, then you can look for attacks. Takeaway: Develop your pieces and castle before launching attacks.

I'd play Ne7. Your move grabbed a pawn, which is a natural instinct, but Ne7 is stronger because it creates two threats at once—attacking both the f5 and g6 pawns—while keeping your knight centralized on a powerful square. From e7, your knight dominates the area around Black's king and forces your opponent to respond to the threat before they can activate their own pieces. Takeaway: When ahead, look for moves that create multiple threats rather than just collecting material.

I'd play Na5.

Your move with the knight was perfectly fine, but here's a chance to learn a useful pattern. By playing Na5, you attack White's bishop on c4 while also protecting your b7 pawn — doing two useful things with one move. This is called "multi-purpose" play, and it's a great habit to build. After White responds, you'll have improved your position without creating any new weaknesses.

Takeaway: Look for moves that do more than one thing at a time, like attacking and defending simultaneously.

I'd play Ne7. While taking the pawn on b6 is tempting, Ne7 is the principled choice because it attacks two pawns at once and keeps your knight centralized and active. At your level, winning the b6 pawn gives Black's king time to activate with a check, whereas Ne7 forces Black to respond to immediate threats while you maintain a dominant position. The knight on e7 is also perfectly placed to support your king's escape if needed. Takeaway: When Ahead, prioritize activity and pressure over grabbing additional material—especially when taking gives the opponent counterplay.

I'd play Na5. Your move captured the knight on d5, but after White recaptures, their center pawn remains and your knight has disappeared. Na5 attacks the bishop on c4 while defending your b7 pawn, forcing White to respond to two threats at once. This pinpoints the difference between swapping pieces and using your pieces actively. Takeaway: Look for moves that create problems for your opponent, not just even trades.

I'd play Kd5. This is a great example of doing two good things at once: your king attacks White's e5 pawn while also protecting your own e6 pawn. The student's move Ke3 was perfectly fine—it does attack the f4 pawn—but Kd5 teaches you to look for moves that multitask, putting pressure on your opponent while keeping your own pieces safe. Takeaway: Always ask if your move can both attack and defend at the same time.

I'd play Qxb5. Your move with the bishop isn't bad, but the queen capture is more precise because it wins the same pawn while keeping your light-squared bishop at home—you might need that bishop later to attack the kingside or defend d5. After Qxb5 Qxb5 Bxb5, you're simply up material with a healthy position, whereas your move allows Black to get active with Qf5 before you've fully secured your advantage. Takeaway: When you can win material with either piece, choose the capture that preserves your most useful pieces for the coming fight.

I'd play Ne7. Your capture on b6 wasn't a bad move, but the knight on d5 is a powerful piece that can do even more: it can attack two pawns at once! By moving to e7, you attack the pawn on f5 and the pawn on g6, giving your opponent two problems to worry about at the same time. Takeaway: Look for moves that attack multiple targets at once.

I'd play Ke4. This move is the most principled approach here: you put your king on e4, where it attacks both the pawn on e5 and the pawn on f4, maximizing activity. At this level, simplifying into a winning king and pawn endgame is often the safest path — grab the material, then promote. Your actual move was also fine, but Ke4 gives you more targets right away. Takeaway: When nothing is immediately threatened, activate your king and attack the most targets possible.

I'd play Na5. Your move Nxd5 is perfectly reasonable—it trades off a central knight and is the most natural move—but missed a chance to address two problems at once. Na5 attacks the bishop on c4 while also defending b7, combining development with a concrete threat. After White plays Nxf6+ and you recapture with the queen, your knight remains on a5 doing a useful job, and you've avoided allowing White to capture on d5 with a piece. Takeaway: when a piece can attack an enemy piece while also defending one of your own, that dual-purpose move often beats a routine trade.

I'd play e3. This pawn move attacks Black's pawn on d4, which is the most advanced and dangerous pawn on the board. While your f4 move kicked the queen, the more urgent issue is challenging that d4 pawn before it creates more problems. By playing e3 now, you're learning to ask "what are my opponent's threats?" and addressing them at the source. Takeaway: When your opponent has an advanced pawn, look for ways to challenge or capture it before worrying about other pieces.

I'd play Qxb5. You played Bxb5, which is a good instinct—grabbing that loose pawn is a reasonable idea—but capturing with your queen is even stronger because it's a "double attack." After Qxb5, your queen attacks both Black's bishop on b7 and their queen on d7 at the same time, so no matter what they do, you'll come out ahead. This is a great example of using your most powerful piece to create multiple threats at once. Takeaway: When capturing a loose pawn, check if one piece can do it while ALSO threatening something else.

I'd play Qxb5.

Your move with the bishop isn't bad, but you missed a chance to set a small trap. When you take the pawn with your queen, you're betting that Black will snap back and capture your queen—and if they do, you recapture with your bishop and end up with an extra piece! If Black notices the threat and moves their queen away instead, you still have a healthy pawn plus the initiative. This is a classic "remove the defender" idea where you exploit the fact that the bishop on b7 is doing double duty. Takeaway: Look for captures that force your opponent to choose between losing material immediately or walking into an even bigger problem.

I'd play Ke4. Your Ke3 was perfectly reasonable—it attacks the f4 pawn—but Ke4 does something special: it puts your king on the ideal central square while attacking *both* White's pawns at once. From e4, you're maximizing your king's activity and keeping your options open, whereas on e3 you've slightly restricted yourself. This is exactly the kind of subtle king positioning that decides king and pawn endings. Takeaway: In king and pawn endgames, always ask whether your king can do "two jobs at once" instead of just one.

I'd play Nxh4. Your b3 move was passive when you had a clear opportunity to grab material—the knight can take that pawn on h4 right now, and after the bishop recaptures, your king steps forward to g2, staying active. At your level, you should be constantly scanning for tactical gifts your opponent leaves hanging, especially when they've left a pawn undefounded or only loosely protected. This sequence also relieves any pressure on your f3 pawn and keeps your pieces coordinated.

Takeaway: When you see a loose pawn, take it—especially when you have a follow-up that keeps your pieces safe.

I'd play Nxh4. You've found a free pawn here—Black's h-pawn has no protection, and taking it costs nothing. While b3 isn't a terrible move, it misses this chance to grab material and simplify the position. Standing on h4, your knight also helps protect your f3 pawn, which is a nice bonus. Takeaway: Always scan the board for loose pieces and pawns you can capture for free.

I'd play e3. You're playing actively, which is good, but the most useful active move here is one that challenges Black's central pawn on d4 while keeping your e-pawn mobile for a later push. Your idea should be to provoke something in the center, then follow up with e5 to gain space and restrict Black's pieces. This teaches you to combine attack with flexible preparation. Takeaway: Challenge your opponent's central pawns before committing to a big push.

I'd play Kh6.

This is a great practical choice because it defends both of your pawns on g6 and h7 at the same time, keeping your king safe and your material secure. With your king on h6, you're not giving White easy targets, and you stay active rather than retreating far from the action. Takeaway: When you can, move your king to a square that protects multiple pieces at once.

I'd play e3. This move challenges Black's pawn on d4 while opening lines for your dark-squared bishop, giving you an active role in the center rather than just pushing the queen around. Your f4 move wasn't bad, but it's a good habit to ask: "What pawn break actually improves my position?" — and here, e3 puts pressure on Black's structure and prepares to support your own center. Takeaway: When you have pawn tension, look for the break that activates your pieces, not just the one that chases the queen.

I'd play Nxh4. You saw that the pawn on h4 was there for the taking, but maybe got nervous leaving your f3 pawn less protected — the great news is that this move solves both problems at once! By taking the pawn, you grab material while still keeping an eye on your f3 pawn from the knight's new square, and you force Black to spend time recapturing. Takeaway: A capture is even better when it also handles a defensive duty.
